data.title: PagSeguro Digital (NYSE:PAGS) Margin Decline Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
PagSeguro Digital (NYSE:PAGS) has just wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of R$5.2b and basic EPS of R$1.74, alongside net income of R$502.0m. These figures put fresh numbers on the table for investors watching its profitability profile. Over recent periods, the company has seen revenue move from R$5.0b in Q4 2024 to R$5.2b in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from R$1.93 to R$1.74. Trailing twelve month EPS stands at R$7.18 on revenue of R$19.7b and net income of R$2.1b, setting the context for how its margins are tracking through this latest print.

See our full analysis for PagSeguro Digital.

With those headline figures in place, the next step is to line them up against the main stories around PagSeguro, highlighting where the numbers back the prevailing narratives and where they start to push back against them.

See what the community is saying about PagSeguro Digital

Advertisement

Margins Ease Back From 11.5% To 10.7%

  • Over the last 12 months, PagSeguro earned R$2.1b on R$19.7b of revenue, which works out to a 10.7% net margin compared with 11.5% a year earlier.
  • Bears argue that pressure from competition, Pix adoption and higher rates could keep squeezing profitability, and the recent move from 11.5% to 10.7% margin gives them support. However, trailing earnings of R$2.1b still line up with the more cautious view that margins ease rather than collapse.
    • The bearish narrative talks about margin compression from pricing pressure and regulation, and the 0.8 percentage point margin change over the year is consistent with that concern.
    • At the same time, trailing earnings growth over five years of 14.3% a year shows the business has generated profit growth historically even with that margin step down.
Skeptics point to that 10.7% margin and slower recent growth as a warning sign. Yet they also track how the bear case could unfold in more detail in the 🐻 PagSeguro Digital Bear Case.

Five Year EPS Growth Versus Flat Recent Momentum

  • Over five years, earnings have grown about 14.3% a year, while one year earnings growth is around 0.09%, so the long run trend looks strong next to the recent flattening.
  • Bulls focus on that 14.3% yearly earnings growth and argue that business initiatives can sustain EPS progress, but the latest trailing EPS of R$7.18 and near flat 0.09% one year earnings growth mean the current data only partly backs that optimism.
    • Bullish commentary highlights potential for higher profit margins, yet the most recent margin is 10.7% compared with 11.5% previously, so the current margin trend does not yet reflect that view.
    • However, the fact that EPS over the last year sits at R$7.18 and analysts forecast about 9.7% yearly earnings growth suggests bulls are working from both the multi year record and the forecast path rather than just the latest 12 months.
Bulls point to that multi year EPS growth and a R$7.18 trailing EPS base as a starting point, and you can see how they build their case in the 🐂 PagSeguro Digital Bull Case.

P/E Of 7x And DCF Fair Value Gap

  • With the share price at US$10.09 and a trailing P/E of 7x compared with peers at 21.1x and the industry at 18.6x, plus a DCF fair value of US$21.84, the stock screens as much cheaper than those reference points.
  • Consensus commentary flags this valuation gap as a potential opportunity, yet the same data set also shows trailing net margin easing to 10.7% and forecast revenue growth of about 5.3% a year. This helps explain why some investors might question how quickly that 7x P/E could rerate.
    • The single allowed analyst price target of US$12.48 sits above the current US$10.09 share price, so the market price is below both that target and the indicated DCF fair value.
    • On the other hand, the slower forecast revenue growth compared with the cited US market rate, together with margin compression from 11.5% to 10.7%, gives a clear reason why the discount exists in the first place.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for PagSeguro Digital on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

If this mix of margin pressure, earnings history and valuation gaps leaves you undecided, check the numbers yourself and move quickly to shape your own take. You might find it helpful to compare those views with our breakdown of 5 key rewards.

See What Else Is Out There

PagSeguro’s softer 10.7% net margin, near flat 0.09% one year earnings growth and cautious valuation gap narrative all point to pressure on profitability and momentum.

data.title: Is EPR Properties (EPR) Pricing Reflect Its Strong Multi‑Year Share Price Performance?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether EPR Properties is offering good value at its current price, this article will walk you through what the numbers are really saying about the stock.
  • The shares last closed at US$57.56, with returns of 4.0% over 30 days, 13.4% year to date, 16.4% over 1 year, 83.3% over 3 years, and 67.3% over 5 years. These figures may influence how investors think about both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent attention on EPR Properties has centered on its positioning as a real estate investment trust focused on experiential properties and how that focus fits investor preferences for income and real asset exposure. This backdrop helps explain why the stock's performance over the past few years is on many investors' radar.

Approach 1: EPR Properties Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes the cash that a business is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the entire company could be worth. For EPR Properties, this is based on adjusted funds from operations, which is commonly used for REITs.

On this approach, EPR Properties is currently generating last twelve months free cash flow of about $398.2 million. Analysts and internal estimates project this to rise to $515.1 million by 2030, with a set of annual cash flow projections out to 2035 that are discounted back to today.

When you add up all those discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $117.71 per share. Against the recent share price of $57.56, this implies the stock is trading at roughly a 51.1% discount to that DCF estimate. This highlights a wide gap between the cash flow based valuation and the current market price.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: EPR Properties Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which makes it a useful cross check against a cash flow model.

What counts as a “normal” P/E really depends on two things: growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one is more reasonable.

EPR Properties currently trades on a P/E of 17.56x. That sits close to the Specialized REITs industry average of about 16.09x and below the peer group average of 25.68x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for EPR Properties is 33.20x, which is its view of what the P/E could be given the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it blends those context factors rather than looking at one dimension. Since the current 17.56x P/E is well below the 33.20x Fair Ratio, the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

For Houlihan Lokey, the starting point is its Book Value of $32.92 per share and a Stable Book Value estimate of $38.20 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 4 analysts. On these estimates, the business is expected to generate Stable EPS of $9.31 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from the same 4 analysts.

The model assumes a Cost of Equity of $3.02 per share. Subtracting that from the Stable EPS gives an Excess Return of $6.29 per share. This is consistent with an Average Return on Equity of 24.37%, which is the rate applied to the equity base to estimate those ongoing excess profits.

When those excess returns are capitalised, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $178.44 per share, which implies the stock is 11.8% undervalued relative to the recent price of $157.30.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Houlihan Lokey Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Houlihan Lokey, the P/E ratio is a useful shortcut because it links what you pay per share directly to the earnings that support that share price. It also conveniently reflects what the market is willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what investors expect from the business and how risky they think those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Houlihan Lokey currently trades on a P/E of 24.5x, compared with the Capital Markets industry average of 23.0x and a peer average of 15.8x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the company is 15.4x. data.title: NexGen Wins Key Rook I Approval As Uranium Execution Risks Loom
data.description:
• NexGen Energy (TSX:NXE) has received final approval from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission for its Rook I uranium project.
  • The regulator approved the Environmental Assessment and granted a Licence to Prepare Site and Construct.
  • Construction of Rook I is expected to begin in summer 2026, clearing a key hurdle for the project.

NexGen Energy now has a clear path to start building what is projected to be the world's largest uranium mine, a significant step for both the company and the Canadian nuclear energy sector. The shares last closed at CA$16.97, with a 20.5% gain year to date and a 139.7% return over the past year. Those moves reflect growing investor attention on uranium exposure and NexGen's role in that space.

With full construction now permitted, the focus turns to how NexGen executes on Rook I and manages timing, costs, and community commitments. The project is supported by strong community and Indigenous stakeholder backing, which may be important as the company enters a new phase of capital spending and project delivery.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for NexGen Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on NexGen Energy.

📰 Beyond the headline: 3 risks and 1 thing going right for NexGen Energy that every investor should see.

This approval moves NexGen from a pure development story toward being a future uranium producer, which can change how you think about its risk profile. The company is still loss making, with a full year 2025 net loss of CA$309.68m and no meaningful revenue, so the investment case largely rests on successful execution of Rook I. With construction expected to start in 2026 and run for several years, NexGen will stay a capital spending heavy, pre production name for some time, more comparable to developers like Denison Mines or Global Atomic than established producers such as Cameco.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Rook I remains a single asset project, so delays, cost overruns or technical issues at this mine could have an outsized impact on NexGen.
  • ⚠️ The company reported a CA$309.68m net loss in 2025 and currently generates less than US$1m in revenue, so funding construction and ongoing cash burn is a key risk to watch.
  • 🎁 Final Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission approval reduces a major permitting uncertainty and gives clearer line of sight to construction starting in 2026.
  • 🎁 Rook I is positioned as a large scale, long life uranium project that could make NexGen a material supplier to global utilities once in production.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the main things to watch are NexGen’s detailed capex and construction schedule for Rook I, its cash balance and any additional financing, and progress on offtake agreements with utilities. Earnings calls and updates around the 2026 construction start will likely focus on whether the company can hold to its four year build timeline and manage cost inflation. You may also want to track how NexGen’s positioning compares with peers such as Cameco and Denison Mines as utilities sign long term contracts and as the uranium price moves, because that context often shapes sentiment toward pre production developers.

data.title: Eli Lilly’s Employer Connect Targets Obesity Coverage Gaps And Investor Questions
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) has launched Employer Connect, a new platform aimed at expanding access to obesity care medicines in the U.S.
  • The program allows employers to coordinate benefits with more than fifteen independent administrators to support access to treatments such as Zepbound.
  • Employer Connect focuses on addressing coverage gaps, costs, and support services for employees seeking obesity care.

Eli Lilly, known for its growing obesity care portfolio that includes Zepbound, is putting more focus on how coverage is structured rather than only on the medicines themselves. By building a platform that connects employers with a network of independent administrators, the company is targeting one of the bigger friction points in obesity treatment access in the U.S. For investors watching NYSE:LLY, this move sits alongside broader interest in weight management therapies and employer driven health benefits.

For you as an investor, Employer Connect raises questions about how much employer backed coverage could influence demand patterns for obesity medicines over time. It also brings potential competitive implications, as payers, rival drugmakers, and benefits managers assess whether similar access models or responses are needed. The traction this platform achieves with large and mid sized employers could be an important marker to monitor.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Eli Lilly by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Eli Lilly.

4 things going right for Eli Lilly that this headline doesn't cover.

Employer Connect is essentially an access and pricing channel that sits between Eli Lilly, employers, and pharmacy partners. For you as an investor, the key angle is that it targets one of the main bottlenecks in obesity care, employer and insurer coverage. By giving companies a menu of more than fifteen program administrators and a discounted Zepbound KwikPen price of US$449 for all doses at the network pharmacy level, Lilly is trying to make obesity benefits easier to budget for and simpler to roll out across large workforces.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Eli Lilly Narrative

  • This launch aligns with the view that obesity and diabetes medicines are central to Eli Lilly’s future, and that broader access channels, including employer and direct-to-consumer offerings, can support that cardiometabolic focus.
  • It also highlights a point raised in the narrative around reimbursement pressure, as Lilly is actively working around uneven payer decisions. This underlines how important coverage remains for the long-term story.
  • The employer-focused distribution model, including direct coordination with benefits administrators and pharmacies, adds a layer of commercialization detail that is not fully captured in high-level growth and pipeline discussions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Eli Lilly to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Heavy reliance on GLP-1 and obesity therapies means any change in employer or insurer willingness to fund weight management drugs could have a meaningful impact on revenue concentration.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have highlighted pricing and reimbursement as key risks, and a dedicated employer channel that involves discounts and program fees could increase scrutiny of margin sustainability if costs rise or discounts deepen.
  • 🎁 Employer Connect may support the view that Eli Lilly can widen access to Zepbound in obesity and obstructive sleep apnea, reinforcing its position against competitors such as Novo Nordisk and, to a lesser extent, Pfizer in weight management.
  • 🎁 The program also ties into broader cardiometabolic plans, as success with employer partnerships could be a template for future launches in related indications, supporting the idea of a durable franchise rather than a single product story.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how quickly large and mid-sized employers sign up to Employer Connect, how many keep the benefit in place after the first contract cycle, and whether other drugmakers respond with similar employer-focused models. It is also worth tracking any commentary from Lilly on how much volume is routed through this channel versus traditional insurance pathways, and whether employers report acceptable cost trends or push for lower pricing over time.
